examples.title: Basic Encapsulation with Property Decorators
examples.description: Demonstrates encapsulation using private attributes (name mangling with double underscore) and property decorators to control access to internal data.
examples.explanation:
1. Private attributes use double underscore (__) for name mangling
2. Property decorators provide controlled read/write access
3. The setter validates data before modification (marks must be 0-100)
4. The display_info() method abstracts away internal implementation details
5. External code interacts only through public interfaces, hiding the complexity

examples.title: Bank Account - Abstraction and Encapsulation Combined
examples.description: Advanced encapsulation example showing how banking operations abstract complex internal logic while protecting sensitive data like balance.
examples.explanation:
1. ABC (Abstract Base Class) enforces abstraction - cannot instantiate directly
2. Private attributes (__balance, __transactions) encapsulate sensitive data
3. __generate_account_number is a private method abstracting generation logic
4. Abstract method withdraw() defines a contract - each account type implements differently
5. Property balance is read-only, preventing direct modification
6. get_transaction_history() provides controlled access to private data
7. Name mangling (_BankAccount__balance) allows controlled access to parent private members
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of transactions

examples.title: E-Commerce System - Multiple Levels of Abstraction
examples.description: Advanced example showing multiple layers of abstraction with encapsulation, including decorator pattern and data hiding across a complete e-commerce workflow.
examples.explanation:
1. Product (ABC) defines abstraction - blueprint for all products
2. Electronics extends Product with additional encapsulated attributes (__warranty)
3. ShoppingCart encapsulates __items list - external code cannot modify directly
4. get_items() returns a copy preventing external modification (defensive programming)
5. OrderProcessor abstracts complex order processing into simple process_order() method
6. Multiple levels: Product → Cart → OrderProcessor show progressive abstraction
7. Each class hides internal complexity behind simple public interfaces
8. This pattern is used extensively in real-world applications (Django, Flask, etc.)
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for cart operations, O(1) for order processing
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of items in cart/orders
